questions.question: What is the primary benefit of caching in a read-heavy system?
options: It permanently stores all data so the database can be deleted
It serves repeated reads from a fast copy, reducing both latency and load on the expensive origin
It encrypts data automatically
It guarantees data is always perfectly up to date
questions.explanation: A cache keeps a copy of answers in a fast store so repeated reads skip the slow, hard-to-scale database. This cuts latency (memory vs disk) and, crucially at scale, absorbs read load so the origin only handles the rare miss.

questions.question: Why is caching NOT 'free performance'?
options: Caches are extremely expensive to buy
A cache is a second copy of data, and keeping it consistent with the origin is genuinely hard — stale values cause bugs
Caches make all reads slower
Caches can only store text
questions.explanation: Introducing a cache means data exists in two places that can disagree. Cache invalidation is famously hard; a wrong or stale value can show users deleted posts or incorrect prices. A cache is also a component that can fail and unleash load on the database.

questions.question: A service handles 1,000,000 reads. Going from a 90% to a 99% hit ratio changes origin reads from how much to how much?
options: From 100,000 to 10,000 — a further 10x reduction
From 900,000 to 990,000 — load increases
It makes no difference
questions.explanation: At 90% hit ratio, 10% (100,000) reach the origin. At 99%, only 1% (10,000) do. Each '9' of hit ratio cuts origin load by roughly 10x — which can shrink the database tier dramatically. That nonlinear payoff is why caching is the first lever in read-heavy systems.

questions.question: Why does caching only help when access has 'locality'?
options: Locality means the data is stored nearby on disk
Caching reuses previously fetched answers; if access is skewed (some data far hotter), a small cache captures most reads — with no repeats there's nothing to reuse
Locality refers to the server's physical location
Caching works equally well for perfectly uniform random access
questions.explanation: Caches exploit temporal/spatial locality — the fact that some data is requested far more than the rest. A cache holding the hot set captures most reads cheaply. If every access were a unique value never requested again, there would be nothing to reuse and caching wouldn't help.

questions.question: Which statement about cache layers is correct?
options: Only the database can have a cache
Caches exist at many layers (browser, CDN, reverse proxy, app, distributed, DB), and each layer that serves a request shields everything below it
Having multiple cache layers is always a bug
The closer a cache is to the database, the bigger the win
questions.explanation: Caches appear up and down the stack. A request is satisfied at the first layer holding the answer, and that layer protects all layers beneath it from the load. Generally the closer the cache is to the user (browser, CDN), the larger the latency and load win.
